title: Mao Zedong
summary: The founder of the People's Republic of China, theorist of peasant revolution, and leader of the Chinese Communist Party — whose decades of rule produced land reform, industrialization, and national unification alongside the Great Leap Forward and the Cultural Revolution, two catastrophes that killed tens of millions.
hook: Mao Zedong won a revolution that Marx said was impossible. Marxism held that socialist revolution required an industrial working class. China in 1949 had almost none. Mao's solution — using the peasantry as the revolutionary force, fighting a protracted rural guerrilla war, encircling the cities from the countryside — was a genuine theoretical innovation. So was the catastrophe that followed: a leader who had solved the problem of seizing power turned out to have no reliable method for knowing when to stop using the tools of revolution once power was won.

beginner.theFigure:
Mao Zedong led the Chinese Communist Party to victory in 1949 after two decades of civil war and Japanese occupation. He founded the People's Republic of China. He ruled it until his death in 1976. Under his leadership, China went through land reform, industrialization, famine, and political terror on scales that affected hundreds of millions of people.

He was born in 1893 in Hunan province, the son of a prosperous peasant. He became a communist organizer in his twenties. He survived a decade of near-destruction, led the Long March that kept the party alive, and emerged from WWII and civil war as the undisputed leader of a country of 550 million people.
beginner.worldOfIdeas:
Mao inherited Marxism and transformed it. Classical Marxism held that revolution required an industrial proletariat — the working class created by capitalism. China's working class was small. Its peasantry — 80% of the population — was enormous.

Mao argued that the peasantry could be the revolutionary force. The Communist Party would organize them, arm them, and lead a guerrilla war from rural bases. Over decades, rural areas would encircle the cities. This reversed the standard Marxist assumption that the city leads the countryside. It also produced a theory of 'protracted war' — patient, decade-long struggle rather than sudden uprising — that became a model for revolutionary movements worldwide.
beginner.centralIdea:
Mao's central contribution was the theory of mass line — the idea that revolutionary leadership must derive its policies from the ideas of the masses, systematize those ideas, then return them to the masses. Leaders go among the people, learn what they think and want, process that through Marxist analysis, and then lead the people to act on a clarified version of their own ideas.

In practice, the mass line became something different. During the Cultural Revolution, Mao mobilized the masses — particularly young Red Guards — to attack the party apparatus he felt had become bureaucratic and revisionist. The mass line became a weapon against the party itself, producing a decade of violence and destruction.
beginner.howSpread:
Mao's ideas spread through two channels. The first was military success: the People's Republic's victory demonstrated that peasant revolution worked. Guerrilla movements in Vietnam, Cuba, and across the developing world studied Mao's military writings. Ho Chi Minh and Che Guevara both engaged seriously with Mao's theory of protracted war.

The second was the Little Red Book — a collection of Mao's quotations published in 1964 and distributed to every Chinese citizen during the Cultural Revolution. It became one of the most printed books in history. During the late 1960s it circulated among Western student movements that had little knowledge of and less interest in what was actually happening in China.
beginner.contestedInheritance:
The Chinese Communist Party's official verdict, issued in 1981, holds that Mao was '70% right and 30% wrong.' This formula credits him with revolutionary victory and national unification while acknowledging the Great Leap Forward and Cultural Revolution as errors. Deng Xiaoping designed it to allow the party to claim the legitimacy of Mao's founding role while distancing itself from his catastrophic policies.

Frank Dikötter's archival research substantially challenges this formula by documenting the death tolls with new precision. The scholarly question is whether the catastrophes were products of Mao's specific pathologies or structural features of the system he built.

general.theFigure:
Stuart Schram's decades of work on Mao's thought — including Mao's Road to Power, a multi-volume collection of early writings — establishes the most rigorous scholarly foundation for understanding Mao as an intellectual. Schram argued that Mao was a genuine theorist, not merely a military commander who learned propaganda, and that his adaptation of Marxism to Chinese conditions was intellectually serious rather than merely opportunistic.

Philip Short's Mao: A Life (1999) represents the most balanced single-volume biography in English — taking seriously both his intellectual development and the catastrophic consequences of his rule. Frank Dikötter's trilogy on the Maoist era (Mao's Great Famine, The Tragedy of Liberation, The Cultural Revolution) uses provincial and local archives opened after 1980 to document the death tolls with greater precision than earlier scholarship could achieve, and argues that the famines and violence were not accidental byproducts of failed policy but predictable consequences of deliberate choices.
general.worldOfIdeas:
Mao's theoretical relationship to Lenin is the essential context for understanding his innovations. Lenin had argued that a vanguard party of professional revolutionaries could bring consciousness to the working class from outside. Mao adapted this to the Chinese situation: the party would bring consciousness to the peasantry. But where Lenin's vanguard was urban and industrial, Mao's was rural and guerrilla.

The Long March of 1934-35 — in which the surviving Communist forces retreated thousands of kilometers to escape Nationalist encirclement — was the crucible of Mao's leadership. By the end of the march, which killed tens of thousands, Mao had consolidated control of the party. The march became central to the party's founding mythology: the story of survival through sacrifice that legitimized the party's claim to revolutionary authority. Mao understood that revolutionary mythology was as important as revolutionary theory.
general.centralIdea:
The Great Leap Forward of 1958-62 is the central test case for understanding what went wrong with Mao's governing theory. The campaign mobilized the peasantry to rapidly industrialize China — melting down agricultural tools to make steel, organizing communes on military lines, reporting false harvest figures to avoid punishment. The result was a famine that killed between 30 and 55 million people, now recognized as the largest famine in human history.

The scholarly debate is about causation. Was the famine primarily the product of bad policy (overambitious targets, collectivization removing incentives) or of deliberate indifference to mass death? Jasper Becker's Hungry Ghosts and Dikötter's Mao's Great Famine document that local officials knew people were dying and concealed it, and that grain was exported even as the famine deepened. The evidence supports deliberate information suppression at the local level, with Mao receiving falsified reports — but Mao had created the system that made falsification inevitable.
general.howSpread:
The Cultural Revolution of 1966-76 was Mao's most consequential and most destructive use of mass mobilization. Convinced that the party had become a new bureaucratic class betraying the revolution, Mao mobilized youth — the Red Guards — to attack 'revisionist' elements in the party, universities, and cultural institutions. Schools and universities were closed. Intellectuals were sent to re-education camps or killed. Party leaders including Deng Xiaoping were purged and imprisoned. Liu Shaoqi, Mao's designated successor, was tortured and died in detention.

Roderick MacFarquhar's multi-volume The Origins of the Cultural Revolution is the essential scholarly account — tracing how the intra-party conflicts of the late 1950s and early 1960s produced the conditions for the Cultural Revolution. MacFarquhar's argument is that the Cultural Revolution was not a sudden eruption but the culmination of a decade of political conflict within the party's leadership.
general.contestedInheritance:
The question of how to evaluate Mao's legacy divides along several axes. Chinese official historiography maintains the 70/30 formula. Western liberal historiography, particularly after the archival openings that enabled Dikötter's research, emphasizes the death tolls and argues the formula is a political judgment, not a historical one. Chinese nationalist historiography credits Mao with restoring Chinese sovereignty after the 'century of humiliation' — emphasizing unification and decolonization over the internal catastrophes.

The specific intellectual question is whether Mao's theory — peasant revolution, mass line, protracted war — is separable from the political pathologies that produced the Great Leap Forward and the Cultural Revolution. The optimistic reading holds that the theory was sound and the catastrophes were products of Mao's aging paranoia and the sycophantic system he created. The pessimistic reading holds that the theory's reliance on mobilization, voluntarism, and the overriding of objective conditions was structurally connected to the catastrophes.

advanced.theFigure:
The most important unresolved question about Mao is whether the mass death associated with his rule was the product of his specific psychological pathologies — paranoia, grandiosity, indifference to suffering — or the structural features of the Leninist party-state he built. This is not merely an academic question: it determines whether Maoism was a specific aberration from communist governance or a particularly extreme expression of its tendencies.

Dikötter's archival work establishes that local officials falsified production data, that the party knew famine was spreading and continued grain exports, and that the violence of the Cultural Revolution was directed and organized rather than spontaneous. This evidence supports the structural argument: the party apparatus Mao built rewarded loyalty and punished honesty, making catastrophic policy failures invisible until they became undeniable. But the same apparatus existed under other leaders before and after Mao without producing comparable catastrophes, which gives some weight to the pathology argument.
advanced.worldOfIdeas:
Mao's intellectual relationship to Marxism is genuinely complex in a way that both hagiographers and critics obscure. His 'On Contradiction' and 'On Practice' (both 1937) are serious philosophical texts that engage with Leninist epistemology — the claim that correct ideas come from practice, not from armchair theory. His theory of contradictions holds that every phenomenon contains internal opposites, and that understanding which contradiction is 'principal' determines correct revolutionary strategy.

This philosophical framework had practical consequences. It justified continuous revolution — since every resolution of a contradiction produces new contradictions, there is no stable endpoint. It also justified arbitrary policy reversals — since contradictions shift, yesterday's correct line may be today's revisionism. The same framework that produced genuine analytical flexibility in military strategy produced political instability in governance: Mao could always find a theoretical basis for reversing course, which made long-term planning impossible and personal loyalty to Mao more important than adherence to consistent policy.
advanced.centralIdea:
The Cultural Revolution raises the deepest theoretical problem in the interpretation of Mao's political thought: can a ruler use mass mobilization against the apparatus of his own state? Mao attempted this and produced a decade of chaos. The analytical puzzle is structural: a vanguard party derives its claim to authority from its correct understanding of the masses' interests. If the party becomes corrupt, who has the authority to say so? Mao's answer was that he did — that his personal authority as revolutionary leader transcended the party apparatus. This required bypassing the party's normal decision-making structures and appealing directly to mass emotion.

Hannah Arendt's analysis of totalitarianism — the perpetual motion of mass mobilization as a substitute for stable governance — captures something real about the Cultural Revolution. But it does not quite fit, because Mao's mobilization was directed at the party itself, which Arendt's model treats as the instrument of totalitarian control rather than its target. The Cultural Revolution represents a genuine theoretical anomaly: a totalitarian ruler using the instruments of mass politics against the apparatus of the state he controls.
advanced.howSpread:
The reception of Maoism in the West during the late 1960s represents one of the most striking cases of political projection in 20th-century intellectual history. The Little Red Book became a symbol for Western student radicals who knew almost nothing about its content or context. Jean-Paul Sartre and Simone de Beauvoir praised the Cultural Revolution as a genuine experiment in direct democracy. Michel Foucault visited post-Mao China and was initially admiring.

This reception depended entirely on ignorance of what was actually happening in China. The Cultural Revolution had closed universities, killed or imprisoned hundreds of thousands of intellectuals, and destroyed most of China's cultural heritage. Information about this was available but systematically discounted by Western leftists who found Chinese communism a useful counterweight to Soviet communism, which had been discredited by Khrushchev's 1956 revelations about Stalin. The Maoist moment in Western intellectual life is a case study in how ideological need shapes what evidence gets attended to.
advanced.contestedInheritance:
The post-Mao settlement in China — Deng Xiaoping's 70/30 formula combined with rapid marketization — represents a specific political resolution of Mao's legacy that has institutional consequences for present-day China. The Chinese Communist Party cannot repudiate Mao because its own legitimacy derives from the revolutionary founding he embodied. This constraint shapes what research is permitted, what archives are accessible, and what public memory is allowed.

The intellectual consequence is that the most rigorous scholarship on Mao — Dikötter's trilogy, Jung Chang and Jon Halliday's Mao: The Unknown Story — is produced outside China and cannot be published there. The debate that would normally occur within a scholarly community — about archives, interpretation, methodology — is structurally distorted by the political constraints on Chinese participation. This is itself a legacy of Mao: the party's inability to fully reckon with his rule reflects the institutional features of the party-state he built.

foundation.theStory: Mao matters as the figure who solved the problem of peasant revolution and then created catastrophes by applying revolutionary methods to governance. His career raises the question of whether the tools required to seize power in a vast agrarian country are compatible with the tools required to govern it humanely — and whether the answer says something specific about Mao or something general about revolutionary party-states.
foundation.theDebate: The central debates concern the magnitude and cause of the death tolls under Mao's rule — how many died in the Great Leap Forward famine, how directly Mao bears responsibility, and whether the Cultural Revolution's violence was organized or spontaneous. A related debate concerns the intellectual legacy: whether Mao's theoretical contributions to Marxism are separable from the political pathologies that produced the catastrophes.
foundation.whyItMatters: Mao matters for understanding the Chinese Revolution, the People's Republic, the global communist movement, the relationship between Marxist theory and revolutionary practice in agrarian societies, and the contemporary Chinese political system whose legitimacy still derives partly from Mao's founding role.
questions: Mao's mass line theory held that revolutionary leadership must go among the people, learn their views, then return a clarified version to them. During the Great Leap Forward, peasants who reported crop failures were punished. How did the mass line become a system that punished accurate reporting — and does the answer implicate the theory, the implementation, or Mao's personal character?

The Chinese Communist Party officially rates Mao '70% right and 30% wrong.' This formula was designed for political purposes by his successor Deng Xiaoping. What would a genuinely historical assessment — rather than a politically motivated one — look like? What evidence would it need to engage, and what questions would it need to answer?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: Schram's multi-volume Mao's Road to Power establishes the intellectual biography. Short's Mao: A Life is the essential single-volume biography balancing complexity with readability. Dikötter's trilogy — Mao's Great Famine (2010), The Tragedy of Liberation (2013), The Cultural Revolution (2016) — uses provincial archives to document the catastrophes with new precision and argues for greater direct responsibility than earlier scholarship attributed to Mao. MacFarquhar's Origins of the Cultural Revolution traces the intra-party dynamics. Chang and Halliday's Mao: The Unknown Story is the most polemical treatment — extensively documented but contested for selective use of sources.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: The opening of provincial and county-level archives in China after 1980, combined with declassified Soviet materials, has substantially improved the evidentiary base for Mao scholarship. The main limits are: central party archives remain closed; surviving witnesses have incentives to distort their testimony; and the death toll estimates for the Great Leap Forward famine — ranging from 15 to 55 million — reflect genuine uncertainty in demographic methods rather than scholarly disagreement about whether the famine was catastrophic. The uncertainty is about magnitude, not fact.
interpretation.takingAPosition: A defensible scholarly position on Mao must engage the structural-versus-pathological debate directly rather than choosing sides by assertion. The strongest available position holds that the two explanations are not mutually exclusive: the Leninist party-state Mao built created structural conditions — information suppression, accountability only upward, punishment of honest reporting — that made catastrophic policy failure possible. Mao's specific personal features — his late-period paranoia, his tolerance for mass death, his use of ideological campaigns to resolve intra-party disputes — then drove the system toward its worst outcomes rather than merely its mediocre ones. This position requires engaging Dikötter's archival evidence, Short's contextualizing argument, and the comparative question of why other Leninist states did not produce comparable catastrophes under less pathological leaders.
questions: Mao's theory of protracted war — patient, decades-long guerrilla struggle from rural bases — succeeded in China and influenced revolutionary movements from Vietnam to Cuba. His theory of continuous revolution — permanent mobilization to prevent bureaucratic consolidation — failed catastrophically. Is there a connection between what made the first theory work and what made the second one dangerous?

The Cultural Revolution targeted precisely the people — intellectuals, scientists, party officials — whose skills and knowledge a modernizing state most needs. Mao justified this by arguing they had become a new bureaucratic class. Was there a legitimate problem he was trying to solve, even if his solution was catastrophic? What would a non-catastrophic response to bureaucratic ossification look like within a one-party system?

research.topic: The scale of famine mortality during the Great Leap Forward
research.content: Demographic research — including Judith Banister's China's Changing Population (1987), Basil Ashton et al.'s 1984 Population and Development Review study, and most comprehensively Dikötter's Mao's Great Famine (2010) — has established that the Great Leap Forward famine of 1959-61 killed between 15 and 55 million people, with most scholars converging on 30-45 million. The range reflects genuine uncertainty in demographic methods and the incompleteness of Chinese vital statistics data from the period. What is not in dispute is that the famine was the largest in human history and was directly caused by Maoist policy.

research.topic: The role of provincial archives in revising Mao scholarship
research.content: Since the 1980s, Chinese provincial and county-level archives have become partially accessible to researchers, enabling Dikötter and others to document local implementation of Maoist campaigns with a granularity previously impossible. This has shifted the scholarly balance away from arguments that attributed famine and violence to accidental policy failures and toward arguments for greater direct responsibility. The emerging consensus based on this archival work is that local officials systematically falsified production data under pressure from above, that this was known and tolerated, and that the system Mao built made honesty structurally dangerous.

research.topic: What Mao knew about the Great Leap Forward famine as it unfolded
research.content: The central party archives, which would contain Mao's communications, intelligence briefings, and decision records during 1959-61, remain closed. What can be established from available sources: Mao received reports from provincial leaders that significantly understated mortality; the 1959 Lushan Conference, where Peng Dehuai criticized the Great Leap Forward and was purged, shows that accurate reporting reached Mao and was rejected. What cannot be established without central archives: the precise information Mao had at specific decision points, whether he knew the scale of mortality his policies were producing, and how his private communications differed from his public positions. The four-part unrecoverable standard: the evidence that would resolve this is in central archives; those archives are closed for political reasons; what can be inferred from available sources is that Mao received falsified reports but had evidence of failure that he chose not to pursue; what cannot be known is the precise content of his decision-making at specific moments.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Founding Father
comparativeNarrative.content: The party's official assessment — Mao 70% correct, 30% in error — treats the Great Leap Forward and Cultural Revolution as serious mistakes by an otherwise great leader. This framework credits Mao with ending the century of humiliation, unifying China, restoring sovereignty, and establishing the conditions for subsequent development. The 30% error acknowledges the catastrophes without treating them as the defining feature of his rule. This assessment is politically constrained — the party cannot repudiate Mao without undermining its own founding legitimacy — and it is institutionally enforced through censorship of more critical scholarship.

comparativeNarrative.perspective: Survivors and victims of the Cultural Revolution
comparativeNarrative.name: The Catastrophe
comparativeNarrative.content: For the estimated tens of millions who were imprisoned, tortured, killed, or lost family members during the Great Leap Forward and Cultural Revolution, the 70/30 formula is inadequate to the point of obscenity. The memoirs of survivors — Jung Chang's Wild Swans, Anchee Min's Red Azalea, Nien Cheng's Life and Death in Shanghai — document the personal experience of the catastrophes at a granularity that aggregate death tolls cannot convey. This tradition of witness literature establishes that the violence was experienced as organized and deliberate, not as accidental byproduct of policy failure.

comparativeNarrative.perspective: Post-colonial and Third World revolutionary tradition
comparativeNarrative.name: The Peasant Revolutionary
comparativeNarrative.content: In the context of 20th-century anti-colonial struggle, Mao's theoretical contribution — that peasant societies could make socialist revolution without an industrial proletariat — was genuinely liberating. It offered a path to revolutionary transformation outside the Soviet model and outside the assumption that development had to follow the Western path. Revolutionary movements in Vietnam, Angola, Mozambique, and elsewhere drew on Maoist military theory and political organization. This tradition evaluates Mao primarily through the lens of anti-imperialism and national liberation rather than through the lens of what happened inside China.

rabbitHole.label: Vladimir Lenin
rabbitHole.reason: Mao adapted the Leninist vanguard party model to Chinese conditions and peasant revolution — understanding what he inherited and what he changed requires understanding Lenin's original framework.

rabbitHole.label: Communism
rabbitHole.reason: Mao's revolution was explicitly communist in theory and built its legitimacy on Marxist analysis — understanding communism as a political system is necessary for understanding both Mao's goals and his methods.

rabbitHole.label: The Chinese Revolution
rabbitHole.reason: The twenty-year civil war and anti-Japanese resistance that brought Mao to power is the essential context for understanding how and why the Communist Party won — and what the victory cost.

rabbitHole.label: The Soviet Union under Stalin
rabbitHole.reason: Stalin and Mao were the two dominant figures of 20th-century communism — comparing their systems, their methods, and their catastrophes reveals both the common features of Leninist party-states and the specific differences.

rabbitHole.label: Totalitarianism
rabbitHole.reason: The question of whether Mao's China fits the category of totalitarianism — and in what ways it differs from both Soviet totalitarianism and fascist totalitarianism — is one of the most productive analytical questions in comparative politics.

rabbitHole.label: Decolonization
rabbitHole.reason: Mao's revolution was experienced internationally as part of the broader wave of anti-colonial liberation — his theory of peasant revolution shaped liberation movements across Asia and Africa.

rabbitHole.label: The Cultural Revolution
rabbitHole.reason: The decade of mass mobilization, violence, and destruction that Mao launched against his own party and society is the most extreme expression of his political theory and deserves its own entry.

reference.author: Short, Philip
reference.title: Mao: A Life
reference.annotation: The essential starting point. Short had access to Chinese sources unavailable to earlier biographers and avoids both hagiography and pure condemnation.

reference.author: Dikötter, Frank
reference.title: Mao's Great Famine: The History of China's Most Devastating Catastrophe, 1958–62
reference.annotation: The definitive account of the Great Leap Forward famine. Winner of the Samuel Johnson Prize. Contested at the margins but not in its core findings.

reference.author: MacFarquhar, Roderick
reference.title: The Origins of the Cultural Revolution (3 vols.)
reference.annotation: Three-volume scholarly standard. Essential for understanding the Cultural Revolution as a product of specific political conflicts rather than pure ideological madness.

reference.author: Schram, Stuart
reference.title: Mao's Road to Power: Revolutionary Writings, 1912–1949 (8 vols.)
reference.annotation: The primary source foundation for Mao scholarship. Schram's introductions are themselves important analytical works.

reference.author: Dikötter, Frank
reference.title: The Cultural Revolution: A People's History, 1962–1976
reference.annotation: Complements Mao's Great Famine with the same method applied to the Cultural Revolution. Essential for understanding the second great catastrophe of Mao's rule.

commerce.author: Short, Philip
commerce.title: Mao: A Life
commerce.note: Start here for a single-volume biography that takes both the revolutionary and the catastrophe seriously — the most readable scholarly account.

commerce.author: Chang, Jung
commerce.title: Wild Swans: Three Daughters of China
commerce.note: Start here if you want the human experience of Mao's China from inside — three generations of women across the revolutionary period. Not scholarship but essential testimony.

commerce.author: Dikötter, Frank
commerce.title: Mao's Great Famine
commerce.note: For the Great Leap Forward specifically — the most rigorous account of the famine, using archives that were unavailable to earlier historians.

commerce.author: MacFarquhar, Roderick
commerce.title: The Origins of the Cultural Revolution, Vol. 1: Contradictions Among the People
commerce.note: For the political dynamics that produced the Cultural Revolution — start with volume 1 if you want the intra-party story.

commerce.author: Schram, Stuart
commerce.title: Mao's Road to Power, Vol. 1
commerce.note: For Mao's actual intellectual development — the primary sources with essential scholarly apparatus.

commerce.title: Morning Sun (2003)
commerce.note: The best documentary on the Cultural Revolution — made by former Red Guards who lived it, combining oral history with archival footage.

popularCulture.title: Wild Swans: Three Daughters of China — Jung Chang (1991)
popularCulture.description: Three generations of women across the revolutionary period. Gets right the texture of life under Mao from inside the system — the ideological pressure, the fear, the genuine belief alongside the terror. Gets complicated by Chang's later Mao: The Unknown Story, which critics argue takes the polemical case too far. Wild Swans is testimony; the later book is argument.

popularCulture.title: To Live (1994, dir. Zhang Yimou)
popularCulture.description: Follows a family across four decades of Chinese political upheaval including the Cultural Revolution. Gets right the way ordinary people navigated extraordinary political violence without being heroes or villains — the moral luck of survival. Banned in China, which is itself a form of cultural analysis of the film's accuracy.

popularCulture.title: Mao portraits — Andy Warhol (1972)
popularCulture.description: Warhol's silkscreen series transforms Mao into the same icon-commodity he applied to Marilyn Monroe and Mao Tse-Tung. Gets right that Mao had become a global pop image divorced from political content. Gets wrong — or deliberately avoids — that the image signified mass death as much as revolutionary charisma. The series is itself a document of Western leftist aestheticization of Maoism.

popularCulture.title: The Little Red Book as cultural object
popularCulture.description: Quotations from Chairman Mao (1964) was distributed to every Chinese citizen during the Cultural Revolution and circulated globally among Western student radicals. As a cultural object it represents the most extreme case of a political text received as icon rather than argument — Western readers who waved it had rarely read it and knew almost nothing about its context. Its persistence as an ironic reference continues to separate the symbol from the catastrophe it accompanied.

popularCulture.title: The People's Republic of Desire (documentary, 2018)
popularCulture.description: Documents the lives of contemporary Chinese internet celebrities, providing context for how Mao's legacy operates in present-day China — through nostalgia, nationalism, and the party's ongoing management of the founding myth. Gets right that the Mao question is not historical but present: how the party uses and constrains Mao's image shapes current Chinese political culture.
